description: Architectural patterns for building interaction systems in UE5 — doors, pickups, NPCs, levers

entries.title: Pattern: Interaction System
entries.description: How to structure an interaction system using an interface for interactable actors, a component on the player for detection, and line traces or overlaps for targeting. Works for doors, chests, NPCs, pickups, levers — anything the player can interact with.
entries.whatItIs: A modular interaction architecture: an interface defines WHAT can be interacted with, a component on the player handles HOW interaction is detected and triggered, and each interactable Actor decides its own behavior when interacted with.
whenToUse: Any game where the player presses a button to interact with world objects
When you have many different interactable types (doors, NPCs, pickups, terminals) that each do something different
When you want a consistent 'Press E to interact' prompt across all interactables
whenNotToUse: If interaction is only collision-based (walking over pickups) — just use overlap events directly
If you have exactly one interactable type — a full system adds unnecessary complexity
keyPoints: STEP 1 — Create an IInteractable interface (UInterface in C++). It declares: Interact(AActor* Caller), GetInteractionPrompt() returning FText, and CanInteract(AActor* Caller) returning bool.

STEP 2 — Create a UInteractionComponent (inherits UActorComponent) on the player. It is responsible for: detecting what the player is looking at, showing/hiding the interaction prompt, and triggering interaction on input.

STEP 3 — The InteractionComponent runs a line trace (or sphere trace) forward from the camera each tick (or on a short timer). If it hits an Actor that implements IInteractable, store it as the CurrentTarget.

STEP 4 — When the player presses the interact key, the InteractionComponent calls IInteractable::Interact(PlayerActor) on the CurrentTarget. The component does NOT know what will happen — it just calls the interface.
STEP 5 — Each interactable Actor implements the interface its own way: a door toggles open/close, a chest opens the loot UI, an NPC starts dialogue, a pickup adds to inventory.
STEP 6 — For the UI prompt: the InteractionComponent updates a widget (or broadcasts a delegate) with GetInteractionPrompt() text from the current target. When no target, hide the prompt.
STEP 7 — For multiplayer: the interaction request goes through a ServerRPC. The server validates CanInteract and then executes the interaction.
commonMistakes: Using actor casting instead of an interface — casting to specific types (Cast<ADoor>) creates hard dependencies and breaks when you add new interactable types
Putting the detection logic on every interactable instead of on the player — the player should find targets, not the other way around
Using Tick with line traces at full frame rate — use a timer (0.1s) or only trace when the player moves/looks around
Coupling the interaction prompt UI to specific actors — the prompt should only display text from GetInteractionPrompt(), nothing actor-specific
Forgetting CanInteract checks — doors that are locked, NPCs in combat, pickups already taken should return false
Running interaction logic on the client in multiplayer — always validate on the server
goodPractices: The interface is the contract — any Actor that implements IInteractable is automatically interactive, no registration needed
The InteractionComponent knows nothing about doors, NPCs, or pickups — it just finds IInteractable actors and calls the interface
Each interactable is self-contained — a door knows how to open, a chest knows how to show loot, they don't share logic
Use CanInteract to control state: locked doors, NPCs in combat, one-time pickups
Add an InteractionRange float on the component so designers can tune how far the player can reach
For multiple interaction options (Talk/Trade on an NPC), extend the interface with GetInteractionOptions() returning an array
